posts.caption:
The first Monday in May — a.k.a. the date of the Met Gala, a.k.a. the party of the year, a.k.a. the Oscars of the East Coast — is almost here. The clothes! The celebrities! The money! The chance to comment knowingly on all of the above while in your pajamas.
But this Met Gala is not just any old Met Gala. This Met Gala is celebrating both the next big Costume Institute show and the Costume Institute’s new home: the Condé M. Nast Galleries. Housed in the 11,500-square-foot space that used to be the Metropolitan Museum of Art’s gift shop, the fashion galleries will now be the first thing visitors see when they walk into the Great Hall. In other words: an undeniable declaration that fashion deserves a prime place in the most storied of fine arts institutions.
It’s a big deal, and may be why Beyoncé agreed to be a host for this year’s event, which will be her the first appearance on the Met carpet in a decade. Who else is hosting? Is there a theme? What’s the dress code? Everything you need to know about the 2026 Met Gala is in our guide. Tap the link in our bio to read it. Photos by @damonwinter, @landonnordeman, @kristaschlueter, @vnina
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on May 01, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'Your Guide to the 2026 2026MetGala Met Gala'.

posts.caption:
The cruise shows, those amorphous in-between collections that big brands have turned into traveling extravaganzas for very important clients and even more important social content, kicked off on Tuesday with Chanel.
The current hottest brand in the industry, according to the fashion search engine Lyst (which assesses brands according to multiple criteria including intent to buy, social conversation, speed of sell-through and cultural relevance), Chanel held its show in Biarritz, the French town where the house was founded in 1915. In the front row were the brand ambassadors ASAP Rocky and Nicole Kidman as well as Tilda Swinton, Sofia Coppola and Michaela Coel; watching onscreen was our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman. Even through the glow of her computer, certain elements stood out.
Blazy offered his spin on the little black dress, which turns 100 this year. Newsprint took on a new meaning. And there were ball gowns that seemed destined for next week’s Met Gala steps. Tap the link in our bio to read @vvfriedman's full review of @chanelofficial's cruise collection. Photos by Marc Piasecki/Getty Images, Chanel

posts.caption:
This month, Vogue did something it had never done before, something most people thought it would probably never do: It put two 76-year-old women on its cover. Sure, they weren’t just any 76-year-olds. They were Meryl Streep and the Vogue overseer Anna Wintour, and they were there in a meta way to discuss the mythology around “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” in which Streep plays a version of Wintour. But still, 76.
The most recent round of fashion shows, which our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman attended last month, were notable not only for the almost total lack of size diversity on the runway, but also for the fact that they were, at the same time, taking one giant step forward when it came to another aspect of inclusivity: Age.
The Chanel show opened with 50-year-old Stephanie Cavalli, who was one of 15 models over 40 on its runway. Bottega Veneta had nine older models. Tom Ford, nine (women and men). Givenchy, eight. Balenciaga, five. Louis Vuitton, four. Representation of women of a certain age has never been higher. What’s going on and why now? At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman delves into the striking shift.
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on April 24, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, people standing, magazine, turtleneck and text that says 'Why Fashion Suddenly Loves Older Women 魚'.

For his Spring 2025 collection, @markgong_official was inspired by the beloved #CharlotteYork, marking the conclusion of a multi-season exploration of #SexandtheCity characters.
The runway was staged like her Upper East Side apartment, and the clothes reflected Charlotte’s signature aesthetic of polka dots, florals, gingham, and tailoring. The opening and most iconic look was a cream dress called “the Lily dress,” referencing a scene from the second movie in which Charlotte’s vintage #Valentino skirt is ruined by her daughter. #MarkGong reimagined the skirt turning the paint marks into hand beaded handprint details.

posts.caption:
Before #MirandaPriestly came the real fashion devil: #Cruella, who thanks to oscar winning costume designer Anthony Powell, was redefined as a fashion and film icon in 1996.
Anthony was inspired by #hautecouture, particularly the work of #ThierryMugler. Sharp tailoring, bold silhouettes, and rich runway textures were pushed into parody to reflect #GlennClose’s camp and villainous character. For example, the red feather “flame” dress (see slide 6) uses intricate detailing to create the illusion that she is engulfed in rage. Some of the corsetry was so extreme that Glenn reportedly felt close to fainting during filming.
Famously, Glenn negotiated in her contract to keep all of Cruella’s costumes, something Disney later tried to reconsider after realizing just how expensive and elaborate the designs were. The lasting impact of these outfits shows how costume design has never been the same since.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 28, 2026. May be an image of costume and text that says 'Cruella de Vil Walked, So Miranda Priestly Could Run'.

posts.caption:
Tomorrow will mark #MatthieuBlazy’s first cruise show for #Chanel in the beachside town of Biarritz, a place of great significance to #CocoChanel. With all the buzz around it, i can’t help but think about the infamous spring 2019 beach show.
#KarlLagerfeld covered the Grand Palais in real sand (sourced from a quarry outside Paris which was returned after the show) and a functioning shoreline. The set was completed with a wooden boardwalk, a lifeguard tower, and beach huts. Models walked barefoot through the sand, making the whole thing feel more like a leisurely stroll along the beach than a fashion show.
(Images courtesy of Chanel / Getty / Collector Square / Kim Weston Arnold / Marcus Tondo / Vogue Runway)

posts.caption:
Could #Madonna’s stolen 2006 #Gucci jacket by #FridaGiannini spark a quiet revival? We all know and love #TomFord’s Gucci, but at this point it’s been overdone. Could Giannini’s era be the next interesting Gucci archive for true fashion girlies to pull from?
Frida had a difficult role stepping in after Ford, yet her version of Gucci brought stability and a different kind of glamour. This was the Gucci I grew up with and she made the brand feel polished and aspirational. A major part of her impact came through accessories, strengthening Gucci’s position as a leader in handbags and leather goods.
She loved jewel tones and metallics, and her references to the 1970s set the groundwork for what would come from #AlessandroMichele. While Michele pushed those references into maximalism and eccentricity, Frida’s work feels more archival than theatrical.
What did you think of Frida’s @gucci ?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 24, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Forget Ford, I Want To See Frida's Gucci Again'.

posts.caption:
Seeing #ElsaSchiaparelli’s archive of perfumes at the @vamuseum made me realise…why are (most) luxury perfume bottles so boring now?
#Schiaparelli worked with creative collaborators such as Jean-Michel Frank, #SalvadorDalí, Leonor Fini, and Marcel Vertès to produce bottles and advertisements that were playful, imaginative, and sometimes shocking. Her most famous perfume, Shocking (1937), came in a bottle shaped like a woman’s torso, inspired by #MaeWest. Another example is Snuff, her only men’s perfume, designed in the shape of a pipe (see slide 11)
Today #perfume bottles today feel so minimal, focusing on consistent, recognisable designs. I guess clean, simple bottles are easier to produce, ship, and display, while complex shapes are more expensive to manufacture and more fragile to handle at scale. Culturally, contemporary luxury marketing often prioritises advertising and celebrity endorsements, shifting creativity away from the bottle itself.

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?
Read the full article at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.
#divorce #marriage #culture kim kardashian, all's fair, legal drama shows, revenge dress, divorce party, single women princess diana revenge dress laura wasser

posts.caption:
FIFA has permitted Afghan Women United, a squad composed of refugees from around the world, to represent Afghanistan in official competitions. Afghan women last competed in an international game in 2018. When the Taliban came into power in 2021, women were banned from representing the country in sporting tournaments.
FIFA's governance regulations previously prevented it from officially recognising a team unless they were recognised by their member association, in this case the Afghanistan Football Federation.
However, after years of campaigns for FIFA to provide Afghan women recognition and financial support, the Federation finally agreed.  Now, FIFA can approve the registration of a national or representative team "under exceptional circumstances" to ensure players are not prevented from playing international football due to "situations beyond their control".
Read more at www.shethepeople.tv

posts.caption:
Not every wedding album is just about the bride and groom.
Sometimes, it’s the quiet confidence and charm of the groom’s sister that deserves its own spotlight. ✨
Think of her presence as more than just a supporting role… she’s often the emotion, the energy, and the elegance that flows through every moment. From portraits where she’s adjusting his sehra with a mix of pride and nostalgia, to candid laughs that capture their sibling bond, to those solo frames where her outfit, her grace, and her personality truly shine… each moment tells a story of its own.
There’s something incredibly special about the way she stands beside him… not just as family, but as someone who’s witnessed every version of him before this day. 💫
Because some portraits aren’t just about how they look… they’re about the relationships they quietly hold within them.
So while you plan your wedding album, don’t forget to give her moments the space they deserve.
Which of these portraits would you want to capture? ✨

posts.caption:
Not every wedding moment asks for attention.
Some simply become unforgettable in the way they’re felt. ✨
Under sunlit arches and florals that seemed to bloom just for them, their story moved forward… not loudly, but with a quiet certainty that needed no announcement. Each step down that aisle felt like a pause in time, where the world watched, but the moment belonged only to them.
There was something effortlessly beautiful in the way they held on to each other, not just in celebration, but in reassurance… like they already understood the rhythm of what forever would feel like. And in the laughter that followed, in the glances they exchanged, you could see it wasn’t just joy, it was comfort, it was home.
It’s found in the way two people walk forward together, hand in hand… knowing this is only the beginning of everything that’s yet to come.

Not every bridal look begins with grandeur.

Think of your wedding look not just as a lehenga or saree… but as a fluid story, where structure meets softness, and tradition blends effortlessly with modernity.
Especially when you’re wearing a Tarun Tahiliani creation… every detail feels light, refined, and thoughtfully layered.
Because the outfit may set the tone…
but it’s the finesse that defines it.
The drapes that flow like second skin.
The silhouettes that sculpt without feeling heavy.
The embroidery that whispers instead of shouting.
The veil that adds just the right touch of drama.
It’s never just one element…
It’s the balance between structure and ease. 💫
Because the most unforgettable bridal looks aren’t just worn…
They move, breathe, and evolve with you.
In 2026, it’s less about excess and more about intention, creating a look that feels effortless, elevated, and entirely your own.
So if your bridal look was a composition… would you start with the silhouette or the drape? 🤍

Beneath cascading drapes and petals that fell like quiet blessings, their story unfolded in a space that felt both intimate and infinite… where every ritual wasn’t just tradition, but a vow gently taking shape in real time.

And then, in the quiet act of signing their names, it all became real. Not just written on paper, but etched into something far deeper… a beginning sealed with intention, witnessed by hearts that knew this was more than a ceremony, it was the start of everything.

A note before you comment: STUDIO gives you access to our free 7 day trial of The Studio of Stylist Elixir, our membership community at £20 a month. We also share free guides and resources on Instagram and a free Substack every Wednesday. 💚
Fashion styling has a rate. It always has. The problem is that this industry has never been transparent about what that rate is, and for decades stylists have absorbed the consequences of that silence.
The work starts before the shoot. The expertise takes years to build. The pressure is carried quietly so nobody else has to feel it. And when that work generates commercial value for a brand, a client, or a campaign, it is not a favour. It is a job. And jobs have rates.
We polled over 1,200 stylists across every sector of the industry because we believe that access to this information should not be a privilege. It should be the starting point.
If you are a stylist who has ever quoted blind, undercharged because you were not sure, or struggled to explain your worth to a client, this is for you.
If you are a client, a brand, or someone who has ever wondered what a fashion stylist actually charges and why, this is for you too.
Comment STUDIO and we will send you access to the full rate breakdown inside The Studio of Stylist Elixir. 💚​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption:
I have been thinking about a question I get asked a lot.
“How do you deal with a client who keeps changing their mind?”
The honest answer is: most of them are not changing their mind. They are working out what they want by saying what they do not want. That is just the process. It is rarely personal and almost never about the work you have done.
What you can change is how you manage it. The first brief in writing. A cap on revisions. A short recap email after every conversation. The right question when the change lands. Charging for the time the changes take.
None of this stops a client changing their mind. It stops the changing of their mind from quietly costing you a weekend, a fee, or your patience.
The stylists I work with who used to find this exhausting now find it boring. That is the win.
Comment CLIENT to find out how Stylist Elixir helps fashion stylists handle clients with
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
Burnout in fashion styling is the most expensive thing in the industry that nobody talks about
It is romanticised as dedication. Mistaken for passion. Disguised as hustle. Most stylists hit a wall and do not have the language for what is happening.
The cost is the dream client missed. The rate that dropped. The quality that started slipping. The
friendships that thinned out. The Sundays that disappeared.
Burnout does not just cost you a few weeks. It costs you the career you were trying to build.
Recovery is real. Take a break. Make time for the things you love. Audit the work that drains you. Ask for help. None of it is optional.

posts.caption:
The fashion industry trains stylists to wait for the big win.
The Vogue cover. The celebrity client. The campaign that changes everything. The credit you can put in your bio and feel like you have made it.
The wait is long. The career happens in between.
The reply from the PR you have been chasing. The new contact in the diary. The shoot you pulled off when nothing was working. The boundary you held when it was hard. The lesson from the job that went wrong.
Every one of these is a win. Every one of these is the career being built.
The big ones come. But they come because of the small ones first.

posts.caption:
Reliance Retail’s beauty ambitions are getting harder to miss. The company has acquired Anomaly, the haircare brand co-founded by Priyanka Chopra Jonas, along with its trademarks, brand assets and digital properties, giving it full ownership of the brand’s intellectual property and digital ecosystem.
Financial terms of the deal have not been disclosed. Chopra Jonas is expected to remain associated with the brand as creative director, with a focus on product development, innovation and brand strategy.
Launched in the US in 2021, Anomaly entered India the following year through Nykaa. But in an increasingly crowded beauty market, the brand failed to become a category-defining brand, proving celebrity beauty is no longer enough on its own.
The acquisition now moves the brand into Reliance Retail’s growing beauty system, which include Tira and Sephora India. Under Reliance, the bet is that scale, shelf space and visibility can do what a celebrity alone could not.
For Reliance, Anomaly is another celebrity-backed brand in the portfolio. But the real acquisition is influence over the shelf, the platform and the consumer journey.
The celebrity gets the headline. The real business happens at the shelf.

India’s new binge is microdramas. Two-minute vertical episodes that don’t build, they escalate. Every scene is a hook. Every episode ends before it settles.
Humiliation arcs. Secret billionaires. Revenge loops. Fiction engineered for payoff, not patience.
But what’s more interesting is where these stories are coming from. Not studios, but dashboards. Plots shaped by what performs, rewritten around what keeps people watching.
This is storytelling built inside the scroll. Loud, fast, and hard to exit. Less about what happens, more about what keeps going.

posts.caption:
Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism) is a creative director, stylist, and artist based in Mumbai. Trained as a communication designer, her practice has evolved from graphic design into a richly tactile, multidisciplinary language that sits at the intersection of art, design, craft and narrative building. After practising communication design for seven years, Shah grew creatively restless and quit her job to start a self-initiated project involving foraging botanicals to create plant-based sculptures. “It was instinctive and exploratory. Less about outcome, more about process,” she notes.
It was this impulse toward the tactile that culminated in the founding of Studio Lalala, a practice that is rooted in immersive storytelling. The studio’s body of work now spans installations, creating spatial environments, styling, and designing sculptural objects. At its core, the practice is less about the medium and more about the narrative and approach. “At its core, my work is about storytelling through material. Whether we are creating a window display, styling a shoot, designing an installation, or building absculptural object, the intention is the same: to create an experience that evokes emotion and curiosity.”
Growing up, creativity wasn’t just a hobby for her, but a part of her life, with her father taking her to work and showing her screen printing, offset printing, and various other industrial processes. “My parents always encouraged curiosity and experimentation. Looking back, I realise that my practice is simply an extension of that childhood, of being fascinated by materials, processes, and the magic of making.” This same curiosity now
informs everything she does, whether in installations, object-making, or fantastical environments; the core of the practice is informed by research, process, and deep
exploration. “I really, really enjoy diving into new  materials, learning from artisans, and pushing ourselves into unfamiliar territories. Learning as we go is a fundamental part of the studio’s DNA.”
📸: Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism)
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Established on April 24, 2026 tagging @pri_ism. May be an image of poster, microphone and text that says 'ESTD TALENT with Priyanka shah'.

posts.caption:
Fashion is entering its legacy era. The pace hasn’t slowed, but the focus has shifted. Instead of chasing micro-trends, the industry is leaning into what lasts. Call it the institutional turn. Heritage, permanence, icons.
You see it everywhere. The global rollout of The Devil Wears Prada 2. Fifth Avenue physically transforming for the Met Gala under the “Costume Art” theme. Brands reinforcing their own mythology instead of trying to outrun the algorithm.
At Celine, it’s about building iconography through accessories that actually stick. At Bottega Veneta, it’s sculptural objects—pieces that feel closer to design than trend. And across the board, there’s a pivot toward personal narrative: collections, capsules, and campaigns that read more like memoir than marketing.
The message is clear. In a market saturated with content, the real flex is longevity. Less “what’s next.” More “what lasts.”
#FashionIndustry #LuxuryFashion #MetGala #Celine #BottegaVeneta

posts.caption:
Desire and Demand. Ok. It’s challenging to "play" with fishing nets and plastic shells while masking it as nostalgic branding. Even assuming the collection’s materials are biodegradable (let’s assume the best), they overlook a critical issue: fishing nets are behind about 10% of the plastic polluting our oceans, killing countless marine animals—perhaps even the mermaids once they’ve finished their catwalk stroll and returned to the water.
In our Entangled exhibition, we engaged with these materials too, but from a more confrontational stance. It’s less comfortable, but for us, it’s necessary.
Admittedly, the Chanel collection looks light and carefree. Who wouldn’t rather think of golden beach days in Biarritz than the waste floating just beyond the shore? We’re not immune to the fantasy. But we also believe the industry’s giants should use their voice to highlight the truth. To not just reference destruction, but to resist it. And that can coexist with creating exceptional fashion—it just takes a touch of boldness.

posts.caption:
If you feel like you’ve been seeing Mark Rothko everywhere lately… same.
Across feeds, galleries, and conversations, his work is having a very 2026 moment—and it’s not random. Rothko’s signature color field paintings—those soft, floating blocks of color—were never just visual. They were emotional environments, meant to be felt more than understood.
He was part of the Abstract Expressionist movement, but his work always leaned quieter, more internal. Large-scale canvases that pull you in, asking you to sit with something deeper: tragedy, ecstasy, tension, stillness.
And right now, that kind of emotional space hits differently.
#MarkRothko #AbstractArt #ArtHistory #CulturalMood #TheCitizensPoste
posts.alt_text: Photo by The Citizen’s Poste on April 28, 2026. May be art of poster and text that says 'So, Are We All in a Mark Rothko Mood Right Now?'.

Whether you’re riding that high this Monday or currently in a full wardrobe crisis (and maybe rethinking how hard you went on fashion history during your last date… maybe that’s why they split the bill), wishing you a good week ahead.
Stay tuned.

posts.caption:
Did you know Luca Guadagnino designed a hotel in Rome? The director behind Call Me by Your Name and Challengers made his hospitality debut with Palazzo Talìa, a 16th-century building transformed into a 26-room boutique hotel through his design firm, Studio Luca Guadagnino.
The concept is very on brand: each space feels like a stage set. A monumental floral carpet by Nigel Peake winds through the entrance, the lobby features Gae Aulenti chairs and a dramatic Murano chandelier, and the bar’s silver-tiled counters reflect original 18th-century frescoes.
Guadagnino’s studio designed the common areas and the Terrace Suite, while the remaining rooms were curated by Mia Home Design Gallery and Laura Feroldi Studio, creating a layered mix of cinematic vision and classic Italian interiors.
A hotel, but make it auteur.
#LucaGuadagnino #Rome #PalazzoTalia #Design #LuxuryTravel

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously youthful-looking for a 50-something. He’s trim, his face glows, he’s light on his feet. His body of work is already impressive and curated, and his physical self suggests the same level of care and attention. “You sacrifice a lot and don’t compromise with your daily routine,” says Bajpayee. It’s been 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has now become a habit. His work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there’s been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee’s progression—an unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma’s classic crime drama ‘Satya’ as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he’s at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan’s, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.

posts.caption:
May’s here, and there’s a distinct feeling this month of standing at a crossroads, or perhaps on a platform where trains arrive and depart without clear announcements. Movement is happening all around you, yet internally you’re still. You still feel the instinct to act, but are also aware that not everything deserves your energy. May becomes a month of pause with purpose. It’s almost as if you’re being positioned in the right direction, at the right moment. And how you respond now matters more than how quickly you move.

posts.caption:
In tune with the beige-ification that has marked fashion’s “quiet luxury” phase of the past few years, drinkware has been content with being unassuming—till now. But call it design’s “emperor’s new clothes” moment, or call it the return of gold old maximalism in fashion—what with Demna at Gucci bringing back the Tom Ford era of unabashed sexiness—but we are finally bidding goodbye to boring glassware.
At Salone del Mobile (@isaloniofficial) 2025, Italian glassware brand Traga (@traga.it) launched statement two-toned cocktail glasses that double as candlesticks. Closer to home, candy-hued glassware is adding playfulness to our tables through curvy, colourful coupes and gem-toned carafes. There’s Muun Home’s (@muunhomedecor) pink and amber swirl glasses, Jaypore’s (@jaypore) pastel carafe set and Rayt’s (ray_homes) goblets in every colour of the rainbow that can offer the glassware glow-up for your next at-home party.
At the link in bio, we have handpicked 15 disruptively beautiful glasses that are not content being dinner-table sidekicks.
Image courtesy: @traga.it
@muunhomedecor @jaypore @nicojournal @amaarahome @_glass_forest_ @lobjet

Absolut Creative Commune is back for its fourth edition, and honestly, it’s the creative palate cleanser we didn’t know we needed. Curated by Spryk, the line-up brings together six artists—think everything from clay animation to high-octane CGI—to dive into the ‘Born Colourless’ philosophy and honour the magic of the human hand.
And for those of us who live for the “how-to,” they’re breaking the fourth wall with a podcast that gets into the BTS stuff: the failed sketches, the studio secrets, and the drafts that almost didn’t make it. And well, we are very much here for it.

posts.caption:
Last weekend, global icon, singer, songwriter and entrepreneur Rihanna returned to India for the debut of her much-anticipated Fenty beauty pop-up, in partnership with Tira and Sephora. The beauty brand, valued at a whopping $1.1 billion with over 5,50,000 Google searches in the third quarter of 2025, inked an exclusive collaboration with Reliance Retail in August last year and has been stocking since across all Sephora and Tira outlets. Rihanna’s two-day visit saw her inaugurating the pop-up, ‘Fenty Beauty ki Haveli’. Fashioned like an opulent palace and spread across multiple beauty rooms, the experiential retail concept is on till May 8 at the Phoenix Palladium Mall, Mumbai.
Beginning at the Powder Room, that recreates the old-world charm of arched 𝒉𝒂𝒗𝒆𝒍𝒊𝒔, is a grand display of foundations complete with a 𝒄𝒉𝒂𝒊 bar. If you are visiting, we recommend taking leisurely sips of tea and trying out Fenty Beauty’s diverse shade card of brown skin friendly, climate adaptive foundations formulated by makeup experts.
Next in line at the pop-up is the Drawing Room designed to amp up eye makeup and add some va va voom with the cult favourite mascara. A fun activity awaits customers where you can block-print your own Fenty Beauty tote bags as a keepsake. The Gathering Room, inspired by royal courtyards is our favourite. Perfect your pout with lip-smacking glosses and liners served with a side of 𝒌𝒖𝒍𝒇𝒊. Our pick? The Gloss Bomb Stix in Jaamun Muse for shine with a single swipe.
The Jewellery Room stands out for its mirror displays and 𝒋𝒂𝒂𝒍𝒊 detailing, completing the royal experience with diamond-dust highlighters and body milks.
The star-studded party, attended by celebrities and influencers, was co-hosted by Isha Ambani, Executive Director at Reliance and Bhakti Modi, CEO, Tira. Rihanna wore a chartreuse custom ensemble by Mugler and accessorised it with bangles by Sabyasachi and a 𝒉𝒂𝒂𝒕𝒉𝒑𝒉𝒐𝒐𝒍 by Manish Malhotra. This super star charged immersive launch clearly signals the emerging face of beauty retail—new-age, conceptual and unconventional experiences that go beyond the transactional to engage consumers.
🖋️ @unnatisaini

posts.caption:
The “Made in India” Prada Kolhapuri, released yesterday into an already bristling conversation on cultural appropriation, reveals something about design but more about the poor choice of promotional language. It also reveals that Western brands may love our “inspirational products” but do not treat India as a viable consumer market. These chappals are crafted, we are told, by artisans associated with the government-backed leather industry bodies of Karnataka and Maharashtra: LIDCOM and LIDKAR. Manufactured in India and developed through a Prada-funded skills initiative, they arrive as “inspired by” the Kolhapuri chappal. The semantic choice is a contradiction. If this is a collaboration involving the National Institute of Fashion Technology (NIFT) and Karnataka Institute of Fashion & Leather Technology (KILT), if the production is rooted in Indian legacy, what exactly is being “inspired”? Could it also be called “How to be inspired by what you have always been doing but learn to move ownership to arm’s length”?
From bandhani skirts to jhumkas by brands like Ralph Lauren—Indian design vocabulary is being repeatedly mined, translated, and elevated elsewhere. But rarely addressed to India as a market with agency. Perhaps this is not just misrepresentation, but the luxury world’s worst kept secret. With the safe assumption that Indian consumers will not be the primary buyers of these products—whether Kolhapuris relabelled by Prada or Janpath jhumkas made in Italy—the Indian buyer is being ticked off as irrelevant and as consumer market—India remains peripheral. Read the full piece via the link in bio.
🖋️ @shefatwork
@lidcom_official @n.i.f.t @lidkarkarnataka
#thevofashion #pradakolhapuri #madeinindia
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Voice Of Fashion on April 28, 2026 tagging @prada, and @shefatwork. May be an image of leather, sandals and text that says '55000000000 PRADA 000000 0000000 The Prada Kolhapuri and The Politics of "Inspired by" CENTRESTAGE'.

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on October 23, 2025.

posts.caption:
I thought I’d imagined this film.
Nobody I knew had seen it, nobody ever mentioned it, but it stayed lodged in my memory for decades! That strange, beautiful, fever dream of an animated movie from my Australian childhood.
When I finally looked into its backstory I couldn’t believe what I found.
And then I couldn’t believe the question nobody had thought to ask about it.
Swipe. Then go watch Lindsay Ellis’s (@namebrandlindsay28) brilliant video essay “Did Disney Really Steal Aladdin?” for the full production history.
Then come back and let me know your thoughts! 💭

posts.caption:
I’ve been noticing cerulean everywhere lately. Which is funny, because I spent years working inside the exact machine Miranda Priestly is describing in that scene.
Most people read it as cruelty, but I read it as a supply chain breakdown. Because I lived within it when I worked in the industry.
The cerulean speech is 20 years old and it’s still the most honest thing anyone has put on screen about how fashion actually works.
The sequel is out now and I have thoughts (I’ll be seeing it next weekend!)
Full video on what it was really like working in fast fashion (and why I left) link in bio.
#thedevilwearsprada2 #devilwearsprada #cerulean

posts.caption:
I rewatched Anastasia and then spent three hours finding out everything I didn’t know about it as a kid. Fair warning: this film is not historically accurate, and by the end of these slides you’ll understand exactly why that matters.
Also how did we KNOW the blue dress was velvet without knowing it was velvet?
But first, Meg Ryan had to be convinced to take the role, Liz Callaway sang every song you love, and Jim Cummings voiced two of the most iconic villain songs of the 90s without getting nearly enough credit for either.
The further I went, the more complicated it got. The film is built on a rumour that was already disproven before it came out. It gave the revolution a face so the Romanovs didn’t have to have one. And Anastasia was canonised as a saint in 2000, a detail Western nostalgia tends to skip past entirely.
I still love this film. I’m just sitting with it differently now. What’s your favourite part? 🙊
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on April 22, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'I rewatched Anastasia for the first time since I was a kid. Then I did the research. veronica tucker'.

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.
Photographed by @annieleibovitz, Styled by @therealgracecoddington, Hair by @thebobbymichael and @dmchairmakeup, Makeup by @melissasilvermakeup and @dmchairmakeup, Tailoring by @billbulldresses, Produced by AL Studio, Production Design by @not.thestate. Vogue, May 2026.

posts.caption: Vogue staffers—like many members of the fashion community—were instantly intrigued by the semi-barefoot shoes that @Matthieu_Blazy sent down the @chanelofficial runway this week in Biarritz. Where would one wear these? Who would wear these? How did we get to this phalange-filled moment? At the link in bio, Vogue’s @chrisjallaire, @hannahjacksuhn, @annagranola, and @isuredontknow go tête-à-tête—or, rather, pied-à-pied—on Chanel’s entry into the freaky shoe arena.

posts.caption:
Extra, extra! Read all about it! Yesterday, @Matthieu_Blazy brought the good press to Biarritz for @chanelofficial’s resort 2027 collection. Towards the end of the show, he popped a newspaper print on a ball gown.
Blazy himself noted the long history of splashy news prints on the runways: “The first to do this kind of dress with newspaper were the suffragettes. Then, of course, Schiaparelli, John Galliano, one of my favorites Per Spook, and Jean Paul Gaultier,” he said at his preview. “But the thing that made me want to do it was a beautiful quote by Gabrielle Chanel. She said ‘I love to read newspapers, like men’.”
In 1935, Elsa Schiaparelli created newspaper print textiles with clippings about her work. It was almost seven decades later that John Galliano created ‘The Christian Dior Daily’ that went down the fall 2000 runway and clothed Carrie Bradshaw. And in the years since, we’ve pored over the Emilia Wickstead Times and Stella McCartney’s own press, while prints have abounded from Helmut Lang to Balenciaga. Blazy-era Bottega Veneta even made headline-making tote bags. Swipe for a brief history of fashion’s love of the newsflash and tap the link in bio to see every look from Blazy's latest collection.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 29, 2026. May be an image of magazine, newspaper, dress and text that says 'VOGLE RUNWAY 下年高 JOURNAL EXTRA, EXTRA! FASHION'S OBSESSION WITH NEWSPAPERS GOES WAY BACK'.

Starting May 1, fans of F1 and fashion can get their hands on a limited-edition Nahmias x Formula 1 collaboration. Tap the link in bio for all the details and for a look at the collection.

posts.caption: @PernilleTeisbaek has been traveling to Milan for Salone del Mobile for a few years now. While the event’s perspective on design changes each year, the one constant is the style of those who travel far and wide to attend. It’s a week dedicated to design—and that doesn’t stop with the interiors. The fashion is nuanced (logos and loud colors are rare), and the strongest looks are often subtly pieced together. Unlike fashion week, Salone is not about being seen—it’s about seeing. At the link in our bio, Teisbaek shares her favorite looks from Milan’s design week.

posts.caption:
✨ Creator, Actor, PHENOMENON! ✨ On the June-July cover of Wedding Vows, we present the trailblazing @mostlysane, Prajakta Koli!
From building a YouTube empire to being the lead for Netflix Original Mismatched, from being part of Michelle Obama’s Day Time Emmy winning docu-series to being UNDP India's First Youth Climate Champion, her journey is nothing short of extraordinary.
With her debut romance novel set to launch this year, along with her first Amazon Prime Original Andhera, Prajakta’s journey is a testament to the power of dreams, hard work, and staying true to oneself. She continues to inspire millions with her authenticity, relatability, and unstoppable spirit.

posts.caption:
“𝐇𝐞 𝐰𝐚𝐥𝐤𝐞𝐝 𝐬𝐨 𝐨𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐬 𝐜𝐨𝐮𝐥𝐝 𝐝𝐫𝐞𝐚𝐦. 𝐒𝐨 𝐝𝐨 𝐲𝐨𝐮.
𝐇𝐚𝐩𝐩𝐲 𝐋𝐚𝐛𝐨𝐮𝐫’𝐬 𝐃𝐚𝐲 to every artist, craftsperson and creator behind every shaadi.
Today is yours.
𝑳𝒊𝒈𝒉𝒕𝒔. 𝑪𝒂𝒎𝒆𝒓𝒂. 𝑺𝒉𝒂𝒂𝒅𝒊. 🎬”
Every early morning before the mandap was dressed.
Every late night after the last guest left.
Every moment you poured yourself completely into someone else’s most important day.
You carried it all.
And you made it look effortless.
𝐓𝐨𝐝𝐚𝐲 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐬𝐩𝐨𝐭𝐥𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭 𝐛𝐞𝐥𝐨𝐧𝐠𝐬 𝐭𝐨 𝐲𝐨𝐮.
Not the venue.
Not the couple.
Not the occasion.
𝐘𝐨𝐮. 🌹
To every planner, decorator, photographer, filmmaker, choreographer, caterer, designer and dreamer who makes the magic happen behind the scenes —
we see you.
𝐖𝐕 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐒𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟒 𝐬𝐞𝐞𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮.
𝐂𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐛𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐬 𝐲𝐨𝐮.
𝐀𝐧𝐝 𝐢𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐭 𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐫𝐞𝐥𝐲 𝐟𝐨𝐫 𝐲𝐨𝐮.
𝘓𝘪𝘨𝘩𝘵𝘴. 𝘊𝘢𝘮𝘦𝘳𝘢. 𝘚𝘩𝘢𝘢𝘥𝘪. 🎬
𝘚𝘦𝘱𝘵𝘦𝘮𝘣𝘦𝘳 1, 2 & 3. 2026
𝘏𝘺𝘥𝘦𝘳𝘢𝘣𝘢𝘥 𝘞𝘦𝘥𝘥𝘪𝘯𝘨𝘴 & 𝘍𝘪𝘭𝘮𝘴 𝘌𝘥𝘪𝘵𝘪𝘰𝘯

posts.caption:
Good things take time. Great things take obsession. 🎬 Some things are worth waiting for... 🌹
And this... this is one of those things.
For decades, two industries have been telling the same story. One through mandaps, marigolds and the sacred fire of a thousand rituals. The other through celluloid, spotlights and the magic of a darkened room. Both chasing the same moment. Both crafting the same emotion. Both asking the same question...
How do you make someone feel something they will never forget?
This September, for the first time ever. 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐲 𝐦𝐞𝐞𝐭.
𝐖𝐕 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐒𝐞𝐚𝐬𝐨𝐧 𝟒
𝑾𝒆𝒅𝒅𝒊𝒏𝒈𝒔 & 𝑭𝒊𝒍𝒎𝒔 𝑬𝒅𝒊𝒕𝒊𝒐𝒏.
𝑳𝒊𝒈𝒉𝒕𝒔. 𝑪𝒂𝒎𝒆𝒓𝒂. 𝑺𝒉𝒂𝒂𝒅𝒊. 🎊
Asia’s Largest B2B Wedding Summit returns to the city that understands grandeur like no other.
Hyderabad it is 🌹 Where Nizami heritage meets cinematic legacy. Where every wedding is already a film and every film feels like a wedding.
𝐒𝐞𝐩𝐭𝐞𝐦𝐛𝐞𝐫 𝟏, 𝟐 & 𝟑. 𝟐𝟎𝟐𝟔 🥳
Three days that will change how two industries see each other. And themselves.
The set is dressed. The cast is arriving.
𝐌𝐚𝐤𝐞 𝐰𝐚𝐲. 🎬✨
𝘐𝘯 𝘚𝘵𝘳𝘢𝘵𝘦𝘨𝘪𝘤 𝘈𝘭𝘭𝘪𝘢𝘯𝘤𝘦 𝘸𝘪𝘵𝘩 𝐃𝐞𝐬𝐢𝐠𝐧 𝐛𝐲 𝐑𝐚𝐣𝐚 🌟

posts.caption:
The Great Gatsby. Promising Young Woman. Maestro. There’s no shortage of career highlights for Carey Mulligan. Our April cover star has never shied away from portraying complicated women with rich inner lives, and now, she’s stepping into her messiest role yet. “I was like, ‘Oh, that’s insane and feral. And I loved it,’” Mulligan recalls of reading one particularly unhinged scene in season 2 of Beef, the Emmy-winning dark comedy-drama miniseries in which her character’s marriage devolves into resentment, blackmail, and regret. With the sophomore season hitting Netflix this month and Greta Gerwig’s highly anticipated Narnia adaptation coming later this year, the actress has her hands full with one buzzy project after another. From Mulligan’s real marriage advice to her dream projects, go deeper into our April cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
#matthieublazy first Cruise show for Chanel wasn’t just a debut it was a calculated reset.
Staged in Biarritz, the very place where Gabrielle Chanel built her early empire, the collection leaned heavily into heritage while quietly rewriting it.
Blazy fused classic Chanel codes with a more relaxed, modern energy think beachside elegance mixed with workwear, denim, and fluid tailoring that felt less “untouchable luxury” and more wearable power.
The show carried a strong sense of freedom and inclusivity, with diverse casting and pieces that balanced craftsmanship with ease signaling that his Chanel isn’t about nostalgia alone, but evolution.
posts.alt_text: Photo by WOMENCULTURE on April 28, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, people standing, suit, magazine, blazer and text that says 'FASHION TRENDS A Closer Look at Matthieu Blazy's First Chanel Cruise Show. WOMEn CULTURE URE RE CUL'.

posts.caption:
#jonathananderson just turned Dior bag charms into miniature art pieces and not the safe, predictable kind. These aren’t accessories, they’re objects of curiosity: measuring tapes twisted into surreal forms, robotic creatures, delicate florals, and sculptural oddities that feel half couture, half toy.
What makes them hit is the tension precision craftsmanship meets playful absurdity. It’s Dior heritage filtered through Anderson’s experimental brain, where even a charm becomes a conversation starter.
Most brands treat bag charms as filler. This? It’s storytelling at micro scale.
📸: Dior
posts.alt_text: Photo by WOMENCULTURE on April 28, 2026. May be an image of purse, magazine and text that says 'CD FASHION TRENDS Jonathan Anderson's New Dior Bag Charms Are Peak Creativity. WOMen CULTURE CUL'.

posts.caption:
Back in 2019, #chanel transformed the Grand Palais into a surreal, hyper-real beach for its Spring/Summer show.
Complete with real sand, rolling waves, and a wooden boardwalk, the set blurred the line between fantasy and reality. Models walked barefoot along the shoreline, carrying heels in hand, embodying effortless, sun-soaked elegance.
It was a striking reminder of Chanel’s ability to turn a runway into an immersive world where fashion isn’t just seen, it’s experienced.
📸: Chanel

posts.caption:
The #metgala 2016 explored the relationship between fashion and technology, setting a futuristic tone.
The theme, “Manus x Machina,” highlighted the blend of handcraft and machine-made design in couture.
Celebrities embraced metallics, structured silhouettes, and innovative fabrics on the red carpet.
Standout looks included Claire Danes’ glowing Zac Posen gown and Zendaya’s sleek Michael Kors ensemble.
The night proved how fashion can evolve with technology while still honoring craftsmanship.

posts.caption:
After 283 Grand Prix races, six wins, 39 podiums and three pole positions, Sergio “Checo” Pérez doesn’t have anything left to prove when it comes to his @F1 racing career — but everything left to build.⁠
⁠
Now leading the charge at the Cadillac Formula 1 Team, the Mexican superstar is turning a fresh start into a final statement. ⁠
⁠
"I had unfinished business," he said in WWD's April Weekend cover story. ⁠
Back on the grid and in @TommyHilfiger, Sergio Pérez steps into a new era. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
From sacred tradition to circular fashion.
The Saudi Fashion Commission’s Sustainable Ihram Initiative is transforming millions of discarded pilgrimage garments into recycled textiles — rethinking waste, sustainability and innovation through the lens of faith and culture.
With Hajj and Umrah pilgrim numbers expected to reach 30 million annually by 2030, the initiative aims to turn one of the world’s most symbolic garments into a blueprint for the future of circular fashion.
Read more at WWD.com — #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Happy 95th anniversary to the @empirestatebldg.
On its golden anniversary in 1981, WWD celebrated with the NYC landmark and gave its readers a fun fact: The iconic landmark at 34th and Fifth Avenue was actually once the home of the Waldorf-Astoria, another fashion favorite. The Empire State Building remains a showstopper in its own right, celebrated in feature films, music videos, and as a venue for weddings, parties, runway shows, and fashion shoots. Since its opening in 1931, it has stood as a symbol of why New York is truly an empire state of mind.
Swipe to see it through the years:
1. Empire State Building at 50 years old, 1981.
2. King Kong movie poster inside the Empire State Building, 1981.
3. Margaret Donohue in Yeohlee, spring 1983 ready to wear preview.
4. Empire State Building, 2009
Research and report: @tonymbl
